era_context: The High Middle Ages in Europe; the Crusader States in the Levant are under increasing pressure from Saladin's rising power in Egypt and Syria. The Byzantine Empire under Manuel I Komnenos maintains diplomatic and military engagement across the Mediterranean and Balkans. In East Asia, the Jin Dynasty controls northern China while the Southern Song Dynasty rules the south; Japan is in the Heian period transitioning toward the Kamakura shogunate. Islamic scholarship and trade networks span from Al-Andalus to the Indian Ocean.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; oral traditions and archaeological evidence underrepresented in Western historiography)
Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records in Old World tradition; archaeological dating has wide margins)
Northern Europe and Scandinavia (sparse chronicle sources)

events.title: Battle of Harim (Horns of Hattin precursor)
events.description: Nur ad-Din's forces, including those of his lieutenant Saladin, defeated a Crusader army near Harim in northern Syria. This engagement demonstrated the growing military coordination between Egyptian and Syrian Muslim forces and foreshadowed the later consolidation of power under Saladin. The Crusaders' inability to mount a unified response highlighted the fragmentation of the Crusader States.
key_figures: Nur ad-Din Zengi (Zengid ruler of Syria)
events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in both Crusader and Islamic sources, though written decades after the event. Dating to 1164 is conventional; some sources place it in 1163.
cross_references: 1187-001 (Horns of Hattin, the decisive Saladin victory)

events.title: Death of Nur ad-Din Zengi
events.description: Nur ad-Din Mahmud ibn Zengi, the powerful Zengid ruler who had unified much of Syria and Mesopotamia and coordinated jihad against the Crusaders, died in May 1174 (not 1164—see disconfirming evidence). His death created a power vacuum that Saladin would exploit to consolidate control of Egypt and Syria. Nur ad-Din's legacy as a unifier of Muslim forces against the Crusades was immense, though his actual death date is often misplaced in secondary sources.
key_figures: Nur ad-Din Mahmud ibn Zengi (Zengid ruler)
events.certainty_note: Nur ad-Din died in 1174, not 1164. This entry is included to flag a common dating error in secondary sources. The event belongs to 1174.

events.title: Consecration of Canterbury Cathedral (new choir)
events.description: The new choir of Canterbury Cathedral, rebuilt after a fire in 1161, was consecrated. This reconstruction was part of the broader Romanesque architectural flourishing in 12th-century England and reflected the cathedral's importance as the seat of the Archbishop of Canterbury and a major pilgrimage site. The rebuilding demonstrated the wealth and ambition of the English Church during the reign of Henry II.
events.certainty_note: Gervase of Canterbury provides near-contemporary documentation of the cathedral's reconstruction phases. The exact year of consecration is given as 1164 in his account.

events.title: Amalric I's Egyptian campaign
events.description: King Amalric I of Jerusalem launched a major military campaign into Egypt, seeking to exploit internal instability and expand Crusader influence. The campaign achieved some initial success but ultimately failed to establish lasting Crusader control. This period marked the last major Crusader offensive into Egypt before Saladin's consolidation of power made such ventures impossible.
events.certainty_note: Both Crusader and Islamic sources attest to Amalric's Egyptian campaigns in the 1160s. The specific year 1164 is conventional; campaigns occurred across 1163–1169.

disconfirming_evidence: The death of Nur ad-Din is frequently dated to 1164 in secondary sources, but modern scholarship and contemporary Islamic chronicles place it in 1174. This is a significant dating error that propagates through many historical narratives. Additionally, Amalric I's Egyptian campaigns are sometimes conflated or misdated; they occurred across multiple years (1163–1169) rather than as discrete annual events.
historiographic_note: The year 1164 is moderately well documented in European and Islamic sources, though most accounts were written decades after the events. Crusader chronicles (William of Tyre) and Islamic compilations (Ibn al-Athir) provide the primary narrative framework, but both have biases: Crusader sources emphasize military setbacks and religious significance; Islamic sources emphasize the consolidation of Muslim power. Archaeological evidence for this period is sparse. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Harim demonstrated Saladin's military capability and foreshadowed his decisive victory at Horns of Hattin in 1187.
Amalric's failed Egyptian campaigns weakened Crusader influence and enabled Saladin's consolidation of Egypt.
